sections.title: What Is Investing and Why It Matters
sections.content:
Investing means putting your money into something with the expectation that it will grow in value over time. Unlike saving, where your money sits in a bank account earning modest interest, investing gives your money the potential to grow faster — but it also comes with risk, meaning you could lose some or all of what you put in.

The main reason to invest is to build wealth over the long term. Inflation — the gradual increase in prices over time — means that money sitting in a regular savings account slowly loses purchasing power. If prices rise by 3 percent a year but your savings only earn 1 percent, you are effectively losing ground. Investments like stocks have historically returned an average of about 7 to 10 percent per year over long periods, which outpaces inflation.

You do not need a lot of money to start investing. Many brokerage accounts have no minimum balance, and you can buy fractional shares — small pieces of a stock — for as little as a few dollars. The most important factor is time: the earlier you start, the more your money can benefit from compound growth, where your returns earn their own returns.

sections.title: Stocks: Owning a Piece of a Company
sections.content:
A stock, also called a share or equity, represents a small ownership stake in a company. When you buy stock in a company, you become a shareholder and own a tiny fraction of that business. If the company does well and grows in value, the price of your stock typically goes up, and you can sell it for a profit. Some companies also pay dividends — regular cash payments to shareholders from the company's profits.

Stock prices go up and down based on many factors, including the company's earnings, economic conditions, industry trends, and investor sentiment. This price movement is called volatility. In the short term, stocks can be quite volatile, but over long periods of 10 years or more, the overall stock market has historically trended upward.

You can buy individual stocks through a brokerage account, but picking individual companies requires research and carries higher risk. If one company performs poorly, your entire investment in that stock could lose value. Many financial advisors recommend that beginners start with diversified funds rather than individual stocks to spread out risk.

sections.title: Bonds: Lending Money for Fixed Returns
sections.content:
A bond is essentially a loan you make to a government or corporation. When you buy a bond, the issuer promises to pay you back the original amount (called the face value or principal) on a specific date (the maturity date), plus regular interest payments along the way. These interest payments are called coupon payments.

Bonds are generally considered less risky than stocks because the payments are predictable and you know when you will get your money back. However, they also tend to offer lower returns. Government bonds, especially those issued by the U.S. Treasury, are among the safest investments because they are backed by the full faith of the government. Corporate bonds pay higher interest rates but carry more risk because companies can default, meaning they fail to make payments.

Bond prices move in the opposite direction of interest rates. When interest rates rise, existing bond prices fall, and when rates drop, bond prices go up. This matters if you want to sell a bond before it matures. For most everyday investors, bond funds or bond ETFs (exchange-traded funds that hold many bonds) are a convenient way to add bonds to a portfolio without buying individual bonds.

sections.title: Mutual Funds and ETFs: Diversified Investing Made Simple
sections.content:
A mutual fund pools money from many investors and uses it to buy a diversified collection of stocks, bonds, or other assets. A professional fund manager decides what to buy and sell within the fund. When you invest in a mutual fund, you own a share of the entire pool, which gives you instant diversification — meaning your money is spread across many investments rather than concentrated in just one.

An ETF, or exchange-traded fund, is similar to a mutual fund but trades on a stock exchange like an individual stock. This means you can buy and sell ETF shares throughout the trading day at market prices, while mutual fund shares are only priced once at the end of each trading day. ETFs often have lower fees than mutual funds.

Index funds are a popular type of mutual fund or ETF that simply tracks a market index, like the S&P 500 (a collection of 500 large U.S. companies). Instead of trying to beat the market, index funds aim to match its performance. They tend to have very low fees and have historically outperformed most actively managed funds over long periods. For beginners, a broad market index fund is often recommended as a simple, low-cost starting point.

sections.title: Understanding Risk and Return
sections.content:
In investing, risk and return are closely linked. Generally, investments with higher potential returns also carry higher risk of losing money, and safer investments tend to offer lower returns. This relationship is called the risk-return tradeoff.

Your risk tolerance is how much uncertainty and potential loss you are comfortable with. It depends on factors like your age, income, financial goals, and personality. A younger person with decades until retirement can typically afford to take more risk because they have time to recover from market downturns. Someone close to retirement may prefer safer investments to protect what they have already saved.

Diversification is one of the most effective ways to manage risk. By spreading your money across different types of investments — stocks, bonds, real estate, and cash — you reduce the impact of any single investment performing poorly. Think of it as not putting all your eggs in one basket. A well-diversified portfolio balances growth potential with stability based on your personal risk tolerance and time horizon.

sections.title: Retirement Accounts: 401(k) and IRA Basics
sections.content:
Retirement accounts are special investment accounts that offer tax advantages to encourage long-term saving. The two most common types in the United States are the 401(k) and the IRA (Individual Retirement Account).

A 401(k) is offered through your employer. You contribute a portion of your paycheck before taxes are taken out, which lowers your taxable income for the year. Many employers match a percentage of your contributions — this is essentially free money and one of the best financial benefits available. For example, if your employer matches 50 percent of contributions up to 6 percent of your salary, contributing at least 6 percent means you get an extra 3 percent from your employer.

An IRA is an account you open on your own through a bank or brokerage. A Traditional IRA gives you a tax deduction on contributions now, but you pay taxes when you withdraw the money in retirement. A Roth IRA works the opposite way: you contribute money you have already paid taxes on, but withdrawals in retirement are tax-free. Both types have annual contribution limits. If you are unsure which to choose, a Roth IRA is often recommended for younger workers who expect to be in a higher tax bracket later in life.

sections.title: Common Investing Mistakes to Avoid
sections.content:
One of the biggest mistakes new investors make is trying to time the market — buying when they think prices are about to go up and selling when they think prices will drop. Research consistently shows that even professional investors cannot reliably predict short-term market movements. A better approach is to invest regularly regardless of market conditions, a strategy called dollar-cost averaging. By investing a fixed amount on a regular schedule, you buy more shares when prices are low and fewer when prices are high, which smooths out your average cost over time.

Another common mistake is letting emotions drive decisions. When the market drops sharply, it is natural to feel anxious and want to sell everything. But selling during a downturn locks in your losses. Historically, markets have always recovered from downturns, and investors who stayed the course were rewarded. Patience and a long-term perspective are your greatest advantages.

Finally, watch out for high fees. Even small differences in fees can have a huge impact over decades. A fund charging 1 percent annually may not sound like much, but over 30 years it can eat up a significant portion of your returns compared to a fund charging 0.1 percent. Always check the expense ratio — the annual fee expressed as a percentage of your investment — before choosing a fund.
